lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market seafood processors and packagers that repeatedly provide custom, toll, contract, co-packing, freezing, filleting, cooking, smoking, canning, or packaging services to external harvesters, aquaculture producers, brands, cooperatives, importers, or distributors.
lens.excluded_activities: Processors principally buying catch and selling seafood on their own account, vertically integrated harvest-processing operations without material third-party service revenue, retail fish markets, shared kitchens below the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or seasonal repeat processing for external product owners, generally billed per pound, per lot, per case, or under negotiated toll/co-packing agreements, with added charges for storage, packaging, labeling, certification, and specialty processes.
lens.deviation_from_default: The lens is narrowed because NAICS 311710 combines external custom/toll processors with principal-account seafood manufacturers and integrated harvesters; their eligibility and benefit-retention mechanics differ materially.

a.bridge: BLS provides industry-direct employment shares but not wage-weighted AI task exposure. USDA and NOAA technology pages demonstrate machine vision, deep learning, robotic loading, species identification, freshness measurement, and digital traceability; the estimate maps these capabilities onto the observed occupation mix. It may overstate exposure for highly variable artisanal species and understate it on standardized fillet and packing lines.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 51.76% of industry employment, cutters and trimmers 29.31%, transportation/material moving 19.99%, hand packers 9.24%, and inspectors/testers 2.60%. Vision-guided handling, grading, trimming support, inspection, packing, inventory, scheduling, HACCP records, and traceability expose a large task share, though dexterous cutting, sanitation, cold-chain handling, and exceptions remain difficult.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 311710 task census by species and product form, paired with commercial benchmarks for robotic loading, filleting, shucking, inspection, packing, yield, sanitation, and back-office automation.
caveats: OEWS reports employment rather than task time or wage-weighted exposure.
Technology demonstrations do not establish applicability across the industry's many species, sizes, and product forms.

rho.bridge: USDA and NOAA describe prototype or commercially developing seafood AI technologies, not a representative five-year implementation rate. The range discounts exposure for integration, washdown, cold and wet environments, delicate products, variable morphology, sanitation, HACCP validation, seasonal utilization, and LMM capital constraints. It may understate mature packaging, scheduling, and document automation.
rho.rationale: Digital HACCP records, inventory, scheduling, species/freshness checks, and selected vision inspection are feasible within five years. High-speed robotic handling and cutting face irregular biological inputs, species changes, yield sensitivity, cleaning requirements, short seasons, and limited engineering staff, holding realized implementation below half of exposed opportunity in the base case.
rho.change_evidence: A processor deployment panel by species and plant size showing pilot-to-production conversion, capex, integration time, operating days, uptime, yield, false rejects, sanitation time, labor hours saved, and 24-month persistence.
caveats: One USDA example focuses on blue crab loading and one NOAA device remains under commercial development.
Seasonal plants can have attractive hourly savings but weak annual payback.

e.bridge: NOAA confirms custom processing in regulated fisheries and surveys all seafood processors, while Sea Grant documents rented shared-use processing for external producers; none measures the current share of LMM 311710 firms with material third-party service revenue. The estimate may be high because many processors own the seafood they sell and low because confidential co-packing is not advertised.
e.rationale: Custom, contract, and co-packing seafood operations clearly exist, but the industry also includes principal-account manufacturers, harvest-linked processors, and importers that own product. Shared-use kitchens are often below the EBITDA band, while larger toll relationships are opaque, so eligibility is centered below one-third with a wide range.
e.change_evidence: A census of the 114 estimated LMM firms matching NOAA processor surveys, FDA registrations, permits, labels, customer disclosures, and interviews to quantify third-party toll/custom volume and service revenue.
caveats: The NOAA custom-processing evidence is specific to Alaska crab fisheries.
NOAA processor data are comprehensive for quantity, value, and employment but do not publish the revenue-model split needed here.

s5.rationale: A five-year qualifying-transfer probability near one-fifth is a bounded judgment for often family- or owner-managed regional processors. Permits, cold-chain infrastructure, customer relationships, waterfront access, and skilled labor can attract buyers, while seasonality, quota and catch risk, aging plants, environmental liabilities, working-capital needs, and owner dependence can instead lead to closure or asset sales.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year denominator-based history combining PitchBook/Capital IQ deals, FDA registration and NOAA permit ownership changes, UCC filings, plant closures, bankruptcy records, and interviews with seafood-specialist brokers and lenders.
caveats: No industry-specific qualifying-transfer rate was found.
Permit, vessel, quota, facility, and operating-company transfers can occur separately and may not constitute a qualifying control sale.

q.rationale: Toll and co-packing prices are generally negotiated by unit or batch, so lower labor, waste, and downtime initially accrue to the processor rather than passing through mechanically. Customer re-bids, transparent yield economics, seasonal capacity, competing domestic and foreign processors, and brand purchasing power share gains over renewals; certifications, scarce regional capacity, product complexity, and switching risk support partial retention.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, bids, and invoice panels before and after automation, including yield guarantees, raw-material ownership, index clauses, storage and packaging fees, renewal timing, churn, utilization, and contribution margins.
caveats: Public sources establish processing models and market structure but do not reveal commercial toll-price terms.
Raw-material price, catch volume, exchange rates, and spoilage can dominate measured margin changes.

d5.bridge: OECD-FAO projects global and regional aquatic-product consumption, production, imports, and trade rather than constant-price U.S. outsourced processing demand over exactly five years. NOAA measures current U.S. consumption and import reliance. The estimate translates moderate Americas demand and import growth into eligible processing volume; it may overstate domestic work when seafood arrives fully processed and understate it when imported raw material receives U.S. value-added processing.
d5.rationale: OECD-FAO projects global aquatic-food demand up 12% and Americas imports up 9% over 2026-2035, while NOAA reports 19.1 pounds of U.S. per-capita consumption in 2023 and 80% import reliance. Five-year eligible demand should be modestly positive, offset by real price declines, limited capture growth, climate and quota volatility, and offshore processing.
d5.change_evidence: U.S.-specific 2026-2031 consumption and import projections by product form, customs data separating finished from raw inputs, NOAA processed-product volumes, domestic catch and aquaculture forecasts, and eligible-processor backlog and utilization.
caveats: Global and Americas projections are imperfect proxies for U.S. LMM processors.
Import reliance can support domestic handling or displace domestic preparation depending on product form.

o.bridge: FDA requirements and seafood technology sources establish physical processing, monitoring, records, inspection, quality, and traceability needs but do not measure the future operator-required share. The estimate treats custody, cold chain, cutting, cooking, sanitation, packaging, corrective action, and accountable HACCP implementation as services software cannot supply alone. It may overstate the eligible share if customers integrate or processing shifts offshore.
o.rationale: Seafood preparation requires physical product transformation and cold-chain control, while processors must implement and document HACCP systems and make records available during inspection. AI can automate decisions, documentation, inspection support, and selected handling, but an accountable plant or vessel operator remains necessary within five years.
o.change_evidence: Regulator-reviewed evidence from highly autonomous seafood plants showing unattended production, sanitation and intervention rates, plus customer data on self-processing, offshore processing, and direct-import substitution.
caveats: Operator-required demand can persist while migrating outside the eligible U.S. independent-processor lens.
HACCP obligations vary by product hazard and do not require every process to be manual.

narrative.executive_view: Seafood preparation has a large manual cutting, packing, and material-handling base and credible machine-vision applications. The coherent roll-up lens is narrower: recurring custom, toll, and co-pack processors serving external product owners, not every company that buys catch and sells finished seafood.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve species and freshness verification, inspection, traceability, HACCP documentation, production planning, inventory, and yield decisions, while vision-guided robotics can address loading and selected handling. Species variability, delicate products, wet washdown environments, sanitation, short seasons, and yield sensitivity slow physical automation.
narrative.value_capture: Unit and batch toll fees allow initial retention through lower labor, spoilage, waste, and downtime. Over time, brand customers can re-bid work or demand better pricing, and offshore processors create alternatives; scarce permits, certifications, regional capacity, and switching risk preserve some benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: Custom processing exists across regional fisheries and small aquaculture markets, but the share of LMM firms earning material third-party service revenue is not published. Transfer supply is also uncertain because permits, quota access, vessels, waterfront plants, and operating companies may be owned and transferred separately.
narrative.demand_durability: Seafood consumption and aquaculture are expected to grow modestly, but U.S. import reliance means domestic processing does not move one-for-one with consumption. Catch limits, climate shifts, disease, trade policy, currency, and offshore value-added capacity can create sharp species- and region-specific swings.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The biggest gaps are third-party revenue share, automation performance across species, and qualifying transfer rates. Food-safety failure, spoilage, quota loss, raw-material shortages, seasonal labor, cold-storage costs, wastewater, customer concentration, imports, and working-capital volatility can erase savings.
narrative.principal_driver: A directly observed manual cutting, packing, inspection, and material-handling workforce with emerging vision and robotics tools.
narrative.principal_weakness: Eligible domestic service volume is obscured by principal-account processing, offshore value addition, import dependence, and species-specific seasonality.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311700
sources.supports: Industry-direct employment mix: production 51.76%, cutters/trimmers 29.31%, transportation/material moving 19.99%, hand packers 9.24%, and inspectors/testers 2.60%.

sources.title: Labor and Process Efficiency Through Autonomous Machine Vision Guided Robotic Loading on Food Manufacturing Lines
sources.supports: USDA project uses blue crab processing to demonstrate 3D vision, deep-learning morphology recognition, robotic picking, orientation, and real-world line testing.

sources.title: Next-Gen AI-Driven Multimode Spectroscopy: Transforming Seafood Quality, Purity, and Traceability
sources.supports: NOAA describes AI species identification, freshness estimation, inventory, fraud reduction, and digital traceability, including 95%+ identification accuracy and freshness within one day.

sources.title: Guidance on Inspection and Seafood HACCP Records
sources.supports: FDA requires seafood processors to establish critical control points, monitor them, keep records, maintain written HACCP plans, and provide records during plant inspection.

sources.title: Fisheries of the United States
sources.supports: NOAA reports 2023 U.S. seafood consumption of 19.1 pounds per capita, estimated 80% import reliance, 6.3 billion pounds imported, and $12 billion in domestic edible processed fishery products.

sources.title: OECD-FAO Agricultural Outlook 2026-2035: Fish and Other Aquatic Products
sources.supports: Projects global aquatic-food demand up 12%, Americas imports up 9%, global production up 11%, and real traded-fish prices down 22% by 2035 versus the 2023-2025 base.

sources.title: Analysis of Custom Processing in Bering Sea and Aleutian Islands Crab Fisheries
sources.supports: NOAA regulatory analysis confirms a distinct custom-processing model within seafood processing and its interaction with processor shares and regional delivery requirements.

sources.title: Seafood Processor Survey
sources.supports: NOAA describes its annual comprehensive survey of U.S. seafood processors covering all plant sizes, product quantities, values, product types, and monthly employment.

sources.title: Handbook on Processing Fish for Small-Scale Fish Farmers
sources.supports: USDA-funded Sea Grant handbook documents rented shared-use processing facilities for external fish farmers, client-specific products, value-added services, and associated food-safety and traceability requirements.
